narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in several ways, including your middle-family-income background and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 65.9% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage rates. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. The biggest push toward the failure prediction came from your LSAT score of 34, which falls below the dataset average of 36.7 and signaled to the model a higher risk compared to stronger scorers. Next, your Year 1 school decile of 6—slightly above the average 5.71—still contributed toward failure because it didn't stand out as exceptionally high in early performance. Your middle family income bracket rounded out the top influences, similarly nudging the prediction toward failure rather than overriding other concerns. While your undergraduate GPA and full-time status offered some counterbalance by aligning with successful profiles, they weren't enough to shift the overall outlook. In comparison to typical students, your academics are competitive in GPA but lag in LSAT and early deciles, placing you in a riskier group for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income family—many in similar situations succeed with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above 36.7, strengthening your early-year grades if possible, or seeking programs with bar prep support that value your GPA strengths. Exploring bridge programs or community college pathways could also build a stronger case for admission elsewhere. With persistence, you can reshape this prediction and pursue your legal goals.
